title: Resource Letter QIE-1: Research in quantum information education
authors: Josephine C. Meyer, Simon Goorney, Tunde Kushimo, Zeki C. Seskir
abstract: In celebration of the 2025 UN International Year of Quantum Science and Technology, this Resource Letter surveys the rapidly-growing field of scholarship in quantum information science and engineering (QISE) education. It is primarily written as a guide for educators wishing to get started teaching QISE using research-based teaching methods, as well as for discipline-based education research (DBER) practitioners looking to get started in this field. Topics covered include scoping the field of QISE education, research into student reasoning in QISE, research-based and research-inspired curricular materials from the high school to graduate level, research-based assessments, simulation and gamification tools, and tools for incorporating discussion of the societal and ethical implications of quantum technologies into the classroom.
summary: This resource letter provides a comprehensive guide to the state of Quantum Information Science and Engineering (QISE) education. It surveys best practices for teaching QISE from high school through graduate levels, addressing curriculum design, assessment tools, and ethical implications. The paper is crucial reading for educators seeking to build robust quantum curricula in an expanding field.
main_problem: The paper surveys the rapidly expanding field of Quantum Information Science and Engineering (QISE) education, aiming to guide educators and DBER practitioners.
main_result: It identifies significant gaps in current QISE education literature regarding global access disparities, specific technical skills needed for industry, and pedagogical best practices across K-12 to graduate levels.
method: The methodology involves a comprehensive literature review synthesizing findings from multiple studies using bibliometric analysis, qualitative interviews, and curriculum mapping.
model_or_system: The focus is not on a physical Hamiltonian but rather on the educational model of QISE itself, covering topics like quantum computing, communication, and sensing principles.
key_observables: Student reasoning levels, curricular coverage (K-12 to grad), assessment validity, workforce skill gaps, and ethical awareness.
important_parameters: Educational level (High School vs. Graduate), geographical location (Global North vs. Global South), and pedagogical approach (e.g., project-based learning).
main_assumptions: The field is growing rapidly enough to require a curated resource guide, but the literature base suffers from disciplinary silos.
figures_summary: One figure tracks the growth of QISE scholarship over time, showing an inflection point around 2020-2021.
paper_structure: The structure is a comprehensive survey, covering scoping the field, reviewing pedagogical methods (labs, AI tools), analyzing workforce needs, and concluding with recommendations for future research directions.
why_interesting: While not focused on fundamental physics, it highlights the practical educational challenges associated with emerging quantum technologies, which informs how theoretical concepts must be taught to the next generation of researchers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Comparison of total entries in our dataset vs. Sco- pus indexed resources by year. Note the abrupt change in x axis scale and the inflection point in approximately 2020- 2021. Five resources with a 2026 publication date (typically released as preprints or early access in 2025), are not shown.
